summary: Fowler declares the Gang of Four's Design Patterns book the best ever written on object-oriented design, possibly any style of design. He notes its enormous influence on the software industry, visible in Java and .NET libraries filled with GOF patterns. However, he cautions that it's not an easy read and recommends readers first become comfortable with basic OO design principles. Despite the learning investment required, he argues the book amply rewards the effort. He also reassures non-C++ programmers that the code examples remain accessible to anyone familiar with C-based languages.
key_points: The Gang of Four book is considered the definitive work on object-oriented design
Its patterns have been deeply embedded into major platforms like Java and .NET
The book requires significant effort and prior OO knowledge to fully appreciate
The investment in understanding the material pays substantial dividends
C++ code examples are accessible to programmers familiar with any C-based language
money_quotes: In my view the Gang of Four is the best book ever written on object-oriented design - possibly of any style of design.
This book has been enormously influential on the software industry - just look at the Java and .NET libraries which are crawling with GOF patterns.
Unlike most books, however, it amply pays that investment.
key_insight: The Gang of Four's Design Patterns book, while demanding significant effort to master, remains the most influential and rewarding text on object-oriented design ever written.
